passages.explanation: Don't judge, so that you won't be judged. With whatever judgement you judge, you'll be judged. Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye but don't notice the beam in your own? Hypocrite — first remove the beam from your own eye, then you'll see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's eye. Don't give what is holy to dogs. Don't throw your pearls before pigs, or they'll trample them and turn and tear you to pieces. Jesus isn't banning discernment — he tells you to use it in the very next breath. He's banning the kind of judgement that sees everyone else's flaws while ignoring your own.

Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and it will be opened for you. Everyone who asks receives. He who seeks finds. To him who knocks it will be opened. Which of you, if your son asks for bread, would give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, would give him a serpent? If you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask him? Therefore whatever you desire for people to do to you, do also to them — for this is the law and the prophets. The Golden Rule isn't just an ethical principle floating on its own — it sits on top of a paragraph about God's generosity. You can treat others well because you're not operating from scarcity.

Enter in by the narrow gate. Wide is the gate and broad is the way that leads to destruction, and many are those who enter in by it. How narrow is the gate and how constricted is the way that leads to life, and few are those who find it. Jesus doesn't promise that the kingdom way will be popular. The narrow gate isn't a trick — it's just demanding. Most people prefer the path that asks less.

Beware of false prophets who come in sheep's clothing but inwardly are ravening wolves. You'll know them by their fruits. Do you gather grapes from thorns or figs from thistles? Every good tree produces good fruit, and every corrupt tree produces evil fruit. A good tree can't produce evil fruit, and a corrupt tree can't produce good fruit. Every tree that doesn't produce good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire. You'll know them by their fruits. The test for a teacher isn't their charisma, credentials, or confidence — it's the long-term fruit of their life and influence.

Not everyone who says to me, Lord, Lord, will enter into the Kingdom of Heaven, but he who does the will of my Father in heaven. Many will tell me in that day: Lord, Lord, didn't we prophesy in your name, cast out demons, and do many mighty works? Then I'll tell them: I never knew you. Depart from me, you who work iniquity. This is one of the most sobering passages in the gospels. Religious activity — even spectacular, supernatural religious activity — is not the same as knowing Jesus. Obedience, not performance, is the evidence.

Everyone who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock. The rain came down, the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house — and it didn't fall, because it was founded on the rock. Everyone who hears these words and doesn't do them will be like a foolish man who built on sand. The same storm came, and it fell — and its fall was great. When Jesus finishes, the crowds are astonished at his teaching, because he teaches as one having authority and not as the scribes. The difference between the two builders isn't what they heard — they both heard the same sermon. The difference is what they did with it. Jesus ends the greatest sermon ever preached with a call to action, not applause.
